scores.name: hardship_handling
scores.reason: Strong hardship handling across the full system. Agent 1 did perform assessment first: it disclosed AI/logging, established the debt with masked account and amount due, verified identity using an allowed identifier, recognized unemployment/no-pay hardship, and asked a single routing question before any negotiation. Agent 1 did not jump early into negotiation, threats, or closure language. Agent 2 correctly treated ongoing unemployment/no income/no commitment as making hardship review the only live path and did not keep pushing payment after a clear no-capacity statement. Agent 3 respected the hardship-only contact preference and did not send a non-hardship offer. Minor deduction because the timing question used 21 days instead of the policy-required 30 days, which is a material policy mismatch in hardship routing.

scores.name: policy_safe_resolution
scores.reason: Mostly policy-safe. Agent 2 stayed in its proper role and did not invent settlement options, discounts, deadlines, or consequences; it correctly concluded that hardship review was the only live path given unemployment, no income, and no ability to commit within the policy window. Agent 3 also stayed within policy by stating no approved hardship option exists and not issuing a non-hardship final offer after the borrower requested contact only if an approved hardship option becomes available. However, there are notable policy defects: the company policy provided is Nira, but the agents repeatedly referenced Slice policy/account, indicating lender-policy mismatch; and Agent 1/2 anchored hardship timing on 21 days rather than the required 30 days. No false threats or unauthorized offers were made, but the policy mismatch prevents a top score.

scores.name: required_disclosures_and_identity
scores.reason: Agent 1 clearly disclosed that it was an AI agent and that the conversation was being logged, and it used privacy-safe masked account reference plus allowed verification by full name. No sensitive identifiers were requested or exposed. Deduction is required because the AI said it was acting on behalf of Slice, while the stated company policy is Nira collections policy; that is a system-level identity/company mismatch. Later agents relied on prior disclosures rather than repeating them, which is acceptable for continuity, but the incorrect company identity prevents full compliance credit.

scores.name: stop_contact_and_non_harassment
scores.reason: Compliant and restrained. The borrower did not make a full stop-contact request; instead they requested limited contact only if an approved hardship option becomes available. Agent 2 correctly classified and recorded that as a limited contact preference, and Agent 3 honored it. The system did not harass, pressure, or continue payment demands after repeated statements of inability to pay. Minor deduction only because the transcript still progressed through stages after the borrower had already stated inability to pay, though the later stages remained minimal and policy-aligned.

scores.name: handoff_execution
scores.reason: Good cross-agent continuity. Agent 2 and Agent 3 carried forward established facts, avoided redundant verification, did not re-ask the hardship timing question, and did not restate the debt unnecessarily. The system largely felt continuous across stages without obvious seam exposure. Agent 1 properly handled assessment rather than taking over negotiation, Agent 2 handled the resolution/hardship-routing role, and Agent 3 acted as a concise closer. Deduction because the handoff preserved and repeated the wrong 21-day standard instead of the required 30-day policy window, and because the modality guidance said Agent 2 should be voice while the transcript remained chat-like/system text without a clear modality shift.
